quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 3, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Advanced Nomenclature: Polyatomic Ions and Oxyacids
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Polyatomic Ions, Oxyacid Naming, Transition Metal Compounds, Formula Writing, Special Cases (Mercury(I), Per- Prefix)

questions.question: What is the correct formula for aluminum sulfate?
questions.explanation: Aluminum sulfate has the formula $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$. Aluminum has a +3 charge ($\text{Al}^{3+}$) and sulfate is $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. To balance charges, two $\text{Al}^{3+}$ (+6) combine with three $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ (-6).

questions.question: The compound $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ is named:
questions.explanation: $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ is named ammonium nitrate. It contains two polyatomic ions: ammonium ($\text{NH}_4^+$) and nitrate ($\text{NO}_3^-$). Ammonium nitrite (D) would be $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_2$.

questions.question: Name $\text{HClO}_4$ (oxyacid):
questions.explanation: $\text{HClO}_4$ is named perchloric acid. The pattern for chlorine oxyacids is: $\text{HClO}$ (hypochlorous), $\text{HClO}_2$ (chlorous), $\text{HClO}_3$ (chloric), $\text{HClO}_4$ (perchloric). The 'per-' prefix indicates one more oxygen than the '-ic' form.

questions.question: Which formula represents calcium hydrogen carbonate?
questions.explanation: Calcium hydrogen carbonate is $\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2$. Calcium has a +2 charge, and hydrogen carbonate (bicarbonate) is $\text{HCO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so two are needed. $\text{CaCO}_3$ (A) is calcium carbonate.

questions.question: The compound $\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}$ is named:
questions.explanation: $\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}$ is named tetraphosphorus decoxide. The prefix 'tetra-' indicates four phosphorus atoms, and 'deca-' indicates ten oxygen atoms. The 'a' in deca is often dropped before oxide.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{SnO}_2$?
questions.explanation: $\text{SnO}_2$ is named tin(IV) oxide. Oxygen has a -2 charge, so two oxygens give -4. Therefore, tin must have a +4 charge, indicated by (IV). Stannous oxide would be $\text{SnO}$ (tin(II) oxide).

questions.question: The prefix 'per-' in perchlorate ($\text{ClO}_4^-$) indicates:
questions.explanation: The prefix 'per-' in oxyanions indicates one more oxygen atom than the '-ate' form. For chlorine: $\text{ClO}_3^-$ is chlorate, so $\text{ClO}_4^-$ is perchlorate. Similarly, 'hypo-' indicates one fewer oxygen than '-ite'.

questions.question: What is the formula for iron(II) phosphate?
questions.explanation: Iron(II) phosphate is $\text{Fe}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$. Iron(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Fe}^{2+}$), and phosphate is $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$. To balance charges, three $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ (+6) combine with two $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ (-6).

questions.question: Which compound contains a polyatomic ion with a 1- charge?
questions.explanation: $\text{NaOH}$ contains the hydroxide ion ($\text{OH}^-$), which has a 1- charge. $\text{CaCO}_3$ has carbonate ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$), $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ has ammonium ($\text{NH}_4^+$, 1+), and $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ has sulfate ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$).

questions.question: The correct name for $\text{CoBr}_2$ is:
questions.explanation: $\text{CoBr}_2$ is named cobalt(II) bromide. Bromine has a -1 charge, so two bromines give -2. Therefore, cobalt must have a +2 charge, indicated by (II).

questions.question: The compound $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ is named:
questions.explanation: $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ is named potassium oxide. Ionic compounds do not use prefixes, and potassium (Group 1) only forms one type of ion (+1), so no Roman numeral is needed.

questions.question: The compound $\text{HNO}_2$ is named:
questions.explanation: $\text{HNO}_2$ is named nitrous acid. For oxyacids, the pattern follows the anion name: $\text{NO}_2^-$ is nitrite, so the acid is nitrous acid ('-ite' becomes '-ous'). Nitric acid (A) would be $\text{HNO}_3$ (from nitrate). Hydronitric acid (C) would imply a binary acid pattern (incorrect here), and nitric oxide acid (D) is not a standard name.

questions.question: Which formula represents sodium peroxide?
questions.explanation: $\text{Na}_2\text{O}_2$ is sodium peroxide. It contains the peroxide ion ($\text{O}_2^{2-}$). Sodium ($\text{Na}^+$) has a +1 charge, so two sodium ions are needed to balance the 2- charge of peroxide. $\text{Na}_2\text{O}$ (B) is sodium oxide (containing $\text{O}^{2-}$), and $\text{NaO}$ (A) and $\text{NaO}_2$ (C) do not represent correct charge-balanced formulas for sodium compounds.

questions.question: Which represents the correct formula for mercury(I) chloride?
questions.explanation: Mercury(I) chloride is $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$. Mercury(I) exists as the diatomic ion $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$ (two mercury atoms sharing a +2 charge). Each chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$, so the formula is $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$ (often called calomel).

questions.question: What is the name of $\text{PbSO}_4$?
questions.explanation: $\text{PbSO}_4$ is named lead(II) sulfate. Sulfate ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) has a -2 charge, so lead must have a +2 charge. Lead(II) sulfate is the correct systematic name. Plumbous sulfate is an older name for the same compound.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered advanced nomenclature with polyatomic ions and oxyacids! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review oxyacid naming patterns and transition metal charges.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on polyatomic ion charges and balancing formulas.
scoreMessages.0: Please review polyatomic ions, oxyacid naming rules, and how to write formulas for compounds with transition metals.
